speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is September 4th, 1996. The survivor is Rabbi Alfred Wolf; the interviewer, Renee Firestone, city-state, Los Angeles, California, United States of America. The language of the interview, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is September 4th, 1996. I have the privilege to interview Rabbi Alfred Wolf. My name is Renee Firestone-- R-E-N-E-E F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. Still We are in Los Angeles, California, in the United States, and the language will be English. Good morning, Rabbi Wolf. Would you please introduce yourself and spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Alfred Wolf, A-L-F-R-E-D W-O-L-F.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your given name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born and Eberbach, E-B-E-R-B-A-C-H, in the state of Baden in Germany on October 7th, 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your family, about your father-- what was his name, what was his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is Herman Wolf, who at the time was in the trenches of World War I. He was a co-owner of a-- of a clothing and-- and dry goods store which had been founded by my grandfather, Benjamin Levy. So the name of the firm was Levy and Wolf, and they were a major part of the commercial life of the great city of Eberbach, which at that time had a population of about 7,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [BACKROUND NOISE] Jewish population, about 10, 12 families, maybe 30, 40 people. I am-- I'm not sure of the exact number.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name and what she was doing at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name-- maiden name-- was Regina Levy and she worked with my father and my grandparents in the dry goods store.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far back do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember fairly well back to the early '20s. There's one memory which probably is induced, rather than really remembered, namely that when my father came back at the end of the war in a dirty uniform and a beard, I said, I want this man to go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if your parents were at work, who raised you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my grandmother and my-- my maternal aunt, Aunt Jenny, were the always available babysitters. And I also spent a good deal of time with my grandfather, who probably influenced my life as much as any other member of the family, including my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name and tell us how he influenced your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Benjamin Levy, called Benny by his friends and by the-- by his wife. And he, apart from being the founder and the-- and still the joint owner of the dry goods store, he also could do just about anything that he wanted with his hands. He was a-- he had a-- a good store of-- of tools, which I always borrowed and never seemed to have put back in the right place. He-- apart from being a salesman for his store, he also could fix a broken umbrella. He had his special tools for that. And he could fix almost anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your grandmother and your aunt's names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother was Sophie Levy and my aunt's name was Jenny Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in one home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My parents and I lived in rented apartments for the first 12, 13 years of my life. And after that, we bought a house just two doors from my grandparents and moved the store from the first floor of my grandparents' house into the specially built first floor of our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents and grandparents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: They certainly were observant. My grandfather and grandmother had a strictly kosher home. My grandfather also served as the volunteer [INAUDIBLE], the cantor, of our little synagogue. And after the illness and death of a religious teacher who was the professional Jew of the community, my grandfather was the prayer leader of the congregation until the elimination of the congregation by-- by the expulsion of the Jews in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a rabbi in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no rabbi locally. But of the organization of the Jewish Community of the State of Baden was a district organization, so there was a district rabbi in a town about 20 miles from a Eberbach who would visit maybe twice a year as a matter of routine and more often if there was a need for a wedding or a funeral.

speaker: interviewer
text: What memories do you have of Jewish holidays in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very much part of the annual routine. The holidays were observed in the orthodox fashion by the first two and the last two days of Passover and of Sukkot and the full time of Shavuot and Rosh Hashanah. And the-- Hanukkah was a specially beloved holiday. And following in my grandfather's footsteps, even as a fairly small child, I started making my own set of gifts for every member of the-- of the family. And I arranged all the Hanukkah observances in my own way. They gave me a great deal of freedom and I suppose that had a great deal to do with my eventually wanting to be a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe a Friday night in your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Friday night, especially in my grandparents' home, was mainly a very important family meal, usually with a four course meal with soup and-- and meat then dessert. If my memory serves me right, we usually, the entire family ate at my grandparents' home, rather than eating separately, which we did the rest of the week. And of course, the men went to the synagogue, including this man, even from I suppose age three or four on. And then we came home for the-- for the meal and spent the rest of the evening together. A major problem arose when I wanted to-- to join a [NON-ENGLISH], a gymnastic organization which had its annual exercises on a Friday night. And it was just-- even after that my parents agreed that I could, it was emotionally very difficult for me to leave the home on Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since both your grandmother and mother spent Friday night together, who lit the candles?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father came home from temple and your grandfather, were there any rituals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The-- the first thing my-- my father and I think my grandfather blessed me and then we had the kiddish at home before the-- as we started the meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you started school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started public school today age six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the first day, going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about school, elementary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elementary school was a-- a very important part of-- of everyday life and the-- as you probably know, in Germany the teacher, the herr [GERMAN] the fraulein [GERMAN], was a [GERMAN], a person whom both you as a person and the public respected. I-- I do remember several of the teachers, not all, and they had the problem of-- of dealing with a-- a young student who by age four was able to read fluently and then comes into a first grade where they have to teach the-- the letters of the alphabet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but I always had a good relationship with my teachers, even with teachers whom the rest of the students considered difficult and-- and very strict. Especially I remember my fourth grade teacher, who took a special interest in me, Herr [GERMAN]. And they frequently would give me extra assignments because I was able to work a little faster than others. And what was, I believe, much more important to the-- in the German schools than in American schools, they-- the ranks of the top students were always known and usually, I was the number one student, the primos, as they called the top student.

speaker: interviewee
text: But sometimes a-- a girl that lived in right in my neighborhood would grab that place away from me. But we were very friendly competitors and usually walked to school together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you start school, which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been in 1921 or-- yeah, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you by the time the regime changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17 and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up till that time, did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had almost only non-Jewish friends because I was the only Jewish child of my age in the town. The-- for a while, two brothers from a nearby town who attended school in-- in Eberbach would stay in town over Shabbat because they would not ride the train, which they did on the other days. So I was fairly close to them, but that was strictly a Shabbat friendship. The rest of the time I spent with young people my own age, none of whom was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it a natural thing in your household to bring in people who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin to fear some changes about the relationships that you had with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not until the Nazis actually took over. Now, the-- if you would have asked the question, when did I become aware of anti-Semitism, that was very early. I specifically remember one incident when I probably was no more than five or six years old when I was walking in one direction on a street on the sidewalk. A youngster who lived a few doors away from my grandparents walked in the opposite direction on the other side. He didn't pay attention to where he was going. He stumbled, fell, got up, and then shook his fist at me and says, you damn Jew. Now, that th the first actual event of anti-Jewish feeling that I witnessed, even though I was very much aware of the fact that as Jews we were different and there were people who didn't like Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you have that feeling as a child? If you had non-Jewish friends and you associated with non-Jews, why did you have a feeling that you were different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the feeling that I was different was from the very fact that I went to synagogue and everybody else went to the church. The-- there was no secret of our being Jews. We were not terribly self-conscious about our being Jews, but we were conscious of the fact that has Jews we were different from people who were not Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: When this incident happened with this non-Jewish child, did you talk to your parents about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I told my parents and they said, well, they-- they did not-- were not aware that that family was in any way anti-Semitic and we continued to have a very good relationship with these neighbors. But somehow, this boy had picked up the idea somewhere. It was there. And of course, later on, I became very much aware of the political reality of anti-Semitism because Eberbach was an early stronghold of the Nazi party, starting in the-- certainly by the mid '20s, probably even by the early '20s. The-- the major industry in town was a factory that was owned and certainly controlled by two of Hitler's closest friends and supporters, and Hitler frequently visited town in order to be in consult with those people. And both the president and the manager of the-- of the company became major-- played major roles in the-- Hitler's government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The president of the company was a man by the name of Kepler, K-E-P-L-E-R, who I believe played a role in the Foreign Service under Hitler. And the manager's name was Pleischinger, P-L-E-I-S-C-H-I-N-G-E-R, and I don't remember what post he had under Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this company's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were a chemical factory and they manufactured an ingredient for the making of-- of motion picture and photographic film. And their major customers directly or indirectly were the American film industry. And I remember my father meeting with Mr. Lemley, who was one of the major powers in-- in the film industry, when he visited relatives in Frankfurt and tried to convince him that they should not buy anything from-- from this firm because they were a major support for the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the firm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was called Odin [? Verka, ?] O-D-I-N, named after the great Germanic god, Odin.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become everywhere this part in this organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I would think by the mid or certainly late '20s, this was a well established fact that these were active participants in the Nazi movement, that if you wanted a job there, you had to join the party and if you were physically and age-wise the right person, you had to join the SA.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the party mean at that time the Jewish population? What did you think they were and who they were or what their aim was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their aim certainly was to exclude the Jews from all political, cultural, and commercial influence. The-- and their slogans certainly indicated that they wanted to eliminate the Jews period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews, any-- anybody to the left of the center party or even the center party was not desirable. No, they-- it was very clear that Hitler's book, represented their party goals. One did not take them too seriously because they were still a minority, and even when they became a major party in the elections, the tendency was to laugh, rather than-- to laugh at them, rather than fear them. When the Nazis did take over, my father took a bet with a relative that they would be through within three or four months. Of course, he lost the bet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read my

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you read it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was certainly a different kind of a book, different from almost anything that had been written in the field, and because it was aimed directly at us as Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take it seriously?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the extent that it did affect life in our town, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you read the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'm sure I didn't read it until I was in my teens. But by that time, that was 1928 and they were getting to be a power nationally, even though they had been a power in our town politically for some time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any physical activities of theirs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They-- one-- except during the time that they were prohibited from wearing their uniforms, you'd-- you'd see their uniforms constantly-- either individuals walking to a meeting or they would march with their drum and fife outfits all the time. And of course, once they took over, there was hardly a time when you didn't hear drums.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning, would it be possible for you, for example, to enter one of these meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. After all, it was known that I was a Jew and I wouldn't have been permitted anywhere near there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did it become serious?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in town, it was-- it was serious economically because they-- those who joined the party, of course, would not buy in a Jewish store. The-- and in as much as the major employers, the-- both Odin [? Verka ?] and those who supported this major store were dominated by the Nazi party. They did not do business with Jews. So it-- it was-- it was an important element of daily life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents and grandparents think of moving out of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That thought did not occur to them because in the Weimar Republic, the-- the instability of the-- of national politics was such that we were sure very much in the spirit of my father's bet that parties like the Nazis would come and go and we just had to sit and-- and be patient and they would be sure to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you finally realize that it's not going away?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Hitler had been in-- in power for a-- a few-- a few months, we knew it was very serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember election day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not per se.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about it, where you aware of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, elections were-- dominated the-- the whole atmosphere, very much as a presidential election does here, except that elections of various types happened a little more often in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have a chance to vote?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Jews in-- in the Weimar Republic were very much part and parcel of civic life. There was no exclusion that I was aware of. And I think they were very proud of the fact that they were full citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know whether they voted? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop for a minute to change tapes now.
